kept.text:
@olabrownnee @WorldlyHQ This is completely normal "dancing" fresh beef. After slaughter, the animal's central nervous system stops but nerve endings in the muscles stay active briefly. Salt, cutting, or handling triggers sodium ions that make the muscles spasm and twitch. 

It's a sign the meat is extremely fresh and safe to eat. The movement stops soon as the nerves die off. No parasites or magic involved.

kept.text:
@newguy1234542 @topkekius Ions are atoms or molecules with a net electric charge. Main types: cations (positive, lost electrons) and anions (negative, gained electrons). In the universe, they're key in plasmas—think ionized hydrogen in stars/nebulae, heavy ions in cosmic rays, and charged particles in solar winds or interstellar clouds.

Tations aren't a standard term, but if tying to your riddle on signals/real, ions form via ionization: energy (heat, light, collisions) knocks electrons free, creating charge from neutral matter. The original signal stays rooted in those physical processes.

@realmadsan @AndrewZywiecMD Why the Mitkus study does not work. @AmerAcadPeds 

•  It applies an oral minimal risk level (MRL from ATSDR, based on ingested aluminum) to injected material.
•  It focuses on “dissolved” aluminum ions as the main concern, while downplaying potential effects of persistent particulate adjuvant that hasn’t fully dissolved yet.
•  Some analyses point to math/equation errors or inconsistencies in how half-lives from Priest were plugged in.
•  It doesn’t account well for potential biopersistence of particles in tissues or individual sensitivities (e.g., people with reduced kidney function or allergies to trace residuals).

Aluminum toxicity generally increases with longer persistence in the body, particularly when it accumulates in sensitive tissues like the brain, bones, or other organs over time. This is a well-recognized principle in toxicology: chronic, low-level exposure or slow clearance leads to cumulative damage through mechanisms such as oxidative stress, inflammation, interference with cellular processes (e.g., enzyme function, neurofilament transport), and disruption of metal homeostasis.

A short-term dose may cause immediate effects but can be cleared faster by the kidneys if in soluble form. Prolonged presence (biopersistence) allows ongoing low-level release of aluminum ions (Al³⁺), which can trigger sustained oxidative damage, lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, and neuroinflammation. In the brain, the elimination half-life of aluminum is estimated at around 7 years, meaning it can build up and contribute to delayed or progressive damage.

Insoluble aluminum adjuvants in vaccines like Engerix-B (aluminum hydroxide) and Recombivax HB (amorphous aluminum hydroxyphosphate sulfate) form particles that dissolve slowly at the injection site. These particles are taken up by immune cells (macrophages), which can transport them systemically, leading to longer persistence in tissues.

Animal models confirm slow dissemination and brain accumulation, with effects on behavior, learning, and inflammation.

Regulatory bodies (FDA, CDC, WHO) and reviews often cite Mitkus (and the earlier Keith 2002 model) because:
•  Direct long-term human infant studies on vaccine adjuvant clearance simply don’t exist (as we’ve discussed).
•  The model was an attempt to fill that gap using the best available (limited) data at the time, plus adjustments for infant growth and kidney maturation.

This is circular or insufficient — they’re essentially using weak, extrapolated data to reassure on a topic where stronger direct evidence is not there. Even some official presentations acknowledge that pharmacokinetic data for injected aluminum adjuvants in infants are “sparse and model-dependent.”

kept.text:
Lactoferrin.
Lactoferrin is a multifunctional protein derived from milk with high affinity for iron ions. It is known that iron is necessary for microorganisms to grow and reproduce, so the sequestration of iron significantly reduces their pathogenic potential.

Clinically testing the age moduls of lifespan and other proximities over the trials.
Lactoferrin is a nutrient classically found in mammalian milk. It binds iron and is transferred via a variety of receptors into and between cells, serum, bile, and cerebrospinal fluid. It has important immunological properties, and is both antibacterial and antiviral.
